So time is important, can be important for many applications. And one of the types of scenarios is called streaming, where you're not just making a single point-to -point RPC, but there's a train of data coming between a sender and a receiver. So a train of packets with various timing constraints that might be there. We've changed the situation. It's now multi -packet or multi-message, and there's timing constraints. So we've complicated it in two ways. And a lot of data fits this pattern. in the real world, okay, where you're continually sampling a sensor. It's continually generating data. Or the most typical example is multimedia. Now, it's not as infinite, but for all intents and purposes, a two -hour movie is pretty infinite in terms of network communication. So we have a stream of messages or packets that are semantically related to each other. And sometimes, you know, we call the timing constraint some synchronization or a fancier network term is quality of service. So there's some time constraint that we have. And here are just some examples of time constraints. There might be a required rate at which data should be transported every 10 milliseconds. Sender should send it at that cadence. And maybe it has to be received

and played out with that cadence. You might also have delay, you know, the maximum delay you're willing to tolerate to set up a session, set up a communication between send a receiver. The maximum end-to -end delay between received packets, so how long can you tolerate a gap between successive packets? The minimum end-to-end delay, that's a little weirder, but yeah, maybe you don't want them too fast because it'll overwhelm you. So you may have set a minimum. This is important. You might want to, you know, set the variance between packet into arrivals to be a certain maximum. And that's called a jitter. Okay. So, you know, between A and B can take one millisecond, between B and C might take 1.5. You know, there might be a difference because of network effects. And you can say, I want to limit that this maximum difference. And then you might have a round-trip communication from sender to receiver back to sender, which happens in RPCs. You might want to say, I want to limit or have a certain maximum round-trip delay. The user's interacting with remote data or remote servers and so forth.
